title: Estimating Techniques
summary: How projects estimate cost and duration and how those estimates tighten as work is progressively elaborated. Reorganised into scan-friendly blocks and framed for PMBOK 8's Schedule, Finance and Scope performance domains, with predictive techniques, PERT, and agile relative sizing side by side.
intro: Analogous, parametric, bottom-up and PERT - which to use, and how accurate each is.

sections.heading: Where estimating lives
sections.body: Estimating is not its own performance domain in PMBOK 8. It supports three of the seven domains: Schedule (sequence and size the work over time), Finance (produce the cost baseline and reserves) and Scope (size the deliverables and work packages). Tailor the technique to how much you know and to the delivery approach.

blocks.title: How to use estimates
lines: Schedule domain: estimates size and sequence the work.
Finance domain: estimates become the cost baseline you measure against.
Tailoring: pick the technique that fits the information and the delivery mode.
Predictive leans on cost/duration estimates; agile leans on relative sizing.

sections.heading: Estimate ranges and the cone of uncertainty
sections.body: An estimate is a range, not a single number. Early on you know little, so the range is wide; as detail emerges (progressive elaboration), the range narrows and accuracy improves. Always express an estimate with its range and assumptions, and refine it as better information arrives rather than treating the first figure as fixed.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='100' x2='304' y2='100' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><path d='M46,46 L298,93 L298,107 L46,154 Z' fill='#1F5FA8' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.2'/><text x='52' y='40' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>+75%</text><text x='52' y='168' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>-25%</text><text x='300' y='86' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>+10%</text><text x='300' y='124' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='end'>-5%</text><text x='80' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>ROM</text><text x='268' y='100' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>definitive</text><text x='172' y='184' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>more detail / later in project →</text></svg>
figure.caption: The cone of uncertainty: a wide ROM range early, narrowing to a definitive estimate.

rows.value: Roughly -25% to +75%. Used very early when information is scarce.
Roughly -5% to +10%. Produced later once scope and detail are well understood.

blocks.title: Trap
lines: A number with no range is not an estimate.
ROM is wide (-25 / +75); definitive is tight (-5 / +10).
Range narrows as you move later in the project with more detail.

sections.heading: Methods, when to use, accuracy
sections.body: Match the technique to how much you know. Analogous early, parametric when data is sound, bottom-up when scope is defined and accuracy matters, three-point when there is uncertainty to model.

rows.value: Adjust a similar past project's actuals. Use early with little detail. Fast and cheap but least accurate; a form of expert judgment.
Rate x quantity from a statistical relationship (e.g. cost/sq m x area). Use when historical data is sound and scales. Accuracy depends entirely on the data and model.
Estimate each work package on the WBS, then roll up. Use when scope is well defined. Most accurate but most time- and effort-intensive; needs a defined WBS first.
Risk-weighted average of optimistic, most likely and pessimistic. Use when activities are uncertain. Improves on a single-point guess by modelling the spread.

groups.label: How
lines: Take a similar past project's actual cost/duration.
Adjust for known differences in size, scope or complexity.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Fast, cheap, rough.
Assumes the past project is genuinely comparable.

groups.label: How
lines: Multiply a known rate by a quantity.
Example: hours per unit x number of units.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: More accurate than analogous when data is good.
Only as good as the historical data behind it.

groups.label: How
lines: Decompose to work-package level of the WBS.
Estimate each piece, then roll up to a total.

groups.label: Trade-off
lines: Most accurate; nothing is averaged away.
Most costly; requires a defined scope and WBS.

blocks.title: Cues
lines: "Similar past project" -> analogous.
"Cost per unit" or "rate x quantity" -> parametric.
"Estimate each work package" or "per the WBS" -> bottom-up.
"Optimistic / most likely / pessimistic" -> three-point (PERT).

sections.heading: PERT formulas
sections.body: Three-point estimating models uncertainty by combining three values. The beta (PERT) form weights the most-likely estimate by four.

blocks.title: PERT
blocks.note: O optimistic; M likely; P pessimistic

lines: Expected estimate (beta/PERT).
Most-likely value weighted by 4.
Estimate spread.
Optimistic and pessimistic set the range.

blocks.title: PERT tip
lines: Weight the most-likely value by 4, then divide by 6.
A wider O-to-P gap means a larger standard deviation and more risk.
Use PERT when a single number would hide real uncertainty.

sections.heading: Relative estimating
sections.body: Adaptive teams estimate relative size and effort rather than absolute cost or duration, then let velocity turn size into a forecast. This fits the Schedule domain when requirements change and detail emerges each iteration.

items.text: Relative units of size/effort/complexity, not hours. Often a Fibonacci-like scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8).
Team reveals point estimates simultaneously, discusses outliers, and re-votes to reach consensus.
Group many items quickly by grouping similar-sized stories together (e.g. small / medium / large buckets).
Points completed per iteration; used to forecast how much fits in future iterations.

blocks.title: Agile tip
lines: Points are relative, not hours; don't convert them to time.
Planning poker surfaces hidden assumptions through discussion.
Affinity sizing is fastest for large backlogs.
Velocity x iterations gives the forecast, not a fixed commitment.

sections.heading: Choosing, refining and reserves
sections.body: Pick the technique by the information available; estimates then feed the baseline. Reserves sit on top of your estimates, not inside them.

items.title: Analogous early
lines: When there is little information to work with.

items.title: Parametric next
lines: When you have good historical data and reliable parameters.

items.title: Bottom-up when defined
lines: When scope and the WBS are set and accuracy matters most.

items.title: Add three-point for risk
lines: When activities carry real uncertainty to model.

rows.value: For identified 'known-unknown' risks. Part of the cost baseline. Detail in the Risk Management guide.
For 'unknown-unknowns'. Outside the cost baseline; needs approval to use.

blocks.title: Wrap-up
lines: Pick the technique by the information available.
Estimates feed the baseline; reserves are added on top, not buried inside.
Progressively refine rather than treating the first figure as final.

sections.heading: Estimating with expert input
sections.body:
Expert judgment is useful when the team lacks complete data, faces novel work, or needs a quick reasonableness check. On the exam, treat it as informed input, not a substitute for evidence.

Strong estimates often combine expert judgment with historical information, assumptions, constraints, and team discussion. The project manager should also watch for optimism bias, anchoring, and pressure from influential stakeholders.
sections.tip: If experts disagree, the PM should facilitate clarification and convergence, not simply average opinions or accept the loudest voice.

items.title: Select the right voices
lines: Use people with relevant domain, technical, delivery, or organizational experience.
Include the team members who will perform the work when possible.

items.title: Surface assumptions
lines: Ask what conditions must be true for the estimate to hold.
Document major constraints, exclusions, and dependencies.

items.title: Challenge bias
lines: Compare expert views with actual data from similar work.
Use anonymous or iterative techniques when hierarchy may distort estimates.

sections.heading: Consensus techniques for uncertain work
sections.body:
Consensus-based estimating helps when uncertainty is high and no single person has the full answer. The purpose is not just to produce a number; it is to expose hidden assumptions, risks, and different interpretations of the work.

Delphi-style estimating gathers expert input independently, shares summarized results, and repeats the cycle until the range narrows. Planning poker uses simultaneous team estimates, then discussion and re-estimation when scores differ widely.

groups.label: What
lines: Experts estimate independently, often anonymously, across multiple rounds.

groups.label: Use
lines: Good for complex or sensitive estimates where status or personality could skew input.

groups.label: What
lines: Team members reveal relative estimates at the same time, then discuss differences.

groups.label: Use
lines: Good for agile teams sizing backlog items and building shared understanding.

groups.label: What
lines: Participants allocate votes to show perceived effort, risk, or complexity.

groups.label: Use
lines: Best as a quick signal for discussion, not as a precise cost or duration estimate.

sections.heading: Quality costs in estimates
sections.body:
Cost of quality reminds the project manager that quality work has costs on both sides: preventing defects and dealing with defects. The exam often tests the idea that spending appropriately on prevention and appraisal can reduce more expensive failure costs later.

Quality-related estimates should fit the product risk, stakeholder expectations, regulatory environment, and definition of done. The goal is not zero spending on quality; it is the right balance for value and risk.
sections.tip: External failure costs are usually the most damaging because the customer or end user has already been affected.

rows.value: Costs to avoid defects, such as training, process improvement, and design reviews.
Costs to inspect or test work, such as audits, reviews, and verification.
Costs to fix defects found before delivery, such as rework and scrap.
Costs after delivery, such as warranty claims, support, reputation damage, and lost trust.

sections.heading: Market and vendor-based estimates
sections.body:
When work will be bought from outside the performing organization, the estimate may come partly from the market. Vendor bids, quotes, and proposals can reveal current pricing, capability, assumptions, exclusions, and delivery risk.

The project manager should compare bids on a common basis. A low bid may exclude needed scope, depend on risky assumptions, or shift costs into change requests.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Do not treat the lowest vendor price as automatically best. The PM should evaluate total cost, scope fit, quality, risk, schedule, and procurement rules.

rows.value: Are vendors estimating the same deliverables, acceptance criteria, and exclusions?
What payment terms, warranties, escalation clauses, and change rules are included?
Does the vendor have credible capacity, experience, schedule logic, and risk controls?
Consider integration, oversight, transition, licensing, support, and internal effort.

sections.heading: Flow-based forecasting
sections.body:
Agile and lean teams may estimate delivery by observing actual flow instead of forecasting every item in detail. Measures such as throughput, lead time, cycle time, work in progress, and cumulative flow help the team predict outcomes from real performance.

A cumulative flow diagram can show whether work is moving smoothly. Expanding bands may indicate a bottleneck, too much work in progress, or blocked handoffs.
sections.tip: For adaptive work, the exam may favor empirical forecasting from completed work over detailed early prediction when the backlog is volatile.

items.text: Amount of work completed in a period.
Elapsed time from request to delivery.
Elapsed time from starting work to finishing it.
Work in progress; too much WIP usually slows flow and increases delays.
Cumulative flow diagram; a visual of work counts across workflow states over time.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
